  "weights": {

    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.4",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Which focus should we prioritize to \"make this stronger\"? A) Rhetorical/style strengthening (conciseness, punch, flow) with minimal change to claims, or B) Substantive strengthening of claims (reframing contributions, verifying logic and evidence alignment, and right-sizing novelty/\"first\" claims), even if that tempers language.",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Strengthening may involve reframing the thesis and contribution so the core claim and gap are stated more crisply.",
    "R1.2": "A tighter argument chain can make the case more persuasive by ensuring premises about routing, training, and outcomes lead coherently to the conclusions.",
    "R1.3": "Stronger writing often requires making sure performance and cost claims are directly and transparently tied to the reported results.",
    "R1.4": "Clarifying the delta versus nearest prior work helps substantiate or recalibrate \"first\" claims to avoid fragility.",
    "R1.6": "Sentence- and paragraph-level polish increases clarity, concision, and perceived strength without changing technical content.",
    "RX.1": "We should signal uncertainty where claims depend on limited tasks or judge models to avoid overstating generality.",
    "RX.2": "While making it stronger, we must avoid overclaiming novelty or performance beyond what the artifact supports.",
    "RX.3": "Work must stay within strengthening the provided abstract rather than introducing new experiments or data.",
    "RX.4": "Claims should not encourage unsafe or misleading deployment practices in cost-sensitive LLM serving.",
    "RX.5": "Any strengthened statements should remain traceable to the artifact’s content, figures, and reported numbers."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: Which focus should we prioritize to "make this stronger"? A) Rhetorical/style strengthening (conciseness, punch, flow) with minimal change to claims, or B) Substantive strengthening of claims (reframing contributions, verifying logic and evidence alignment, and right-sizing novelty/"first" claims), even if that tempers language.

rationale.R1.1: Strengthening may involve reframing the thesis and contribution so the core claim and gap are stated more crisply.
rationale.R1.2: A tighter argument chain can make the case more persuasive by ensuring premises about routing, training, and outcomes lead coherently to the conclusions.
rationale.R1.3: Stronger writing often requires making sure performance and cost claims are directly and transparently tied to the reported results.
rationale.R1.4: Clarifying the delta versus nearest prior work helps substantiate or recalibrate "first" claims to avoid fragility.
rationale.R1.6: Sentence- and paragraph-level polish increases clarity, concision, and perceived strength without changing technical content.
rationale.RX.1: We should signal uncertainty where claims depend on limited tasks or judge models to avoid overstating generality.
rationale.RX.2: While making it stronger, we must avoid overclaiming novelty or performance beyond what the artifact supports.
rationale.RX.3: Work must stay within strengthening the provided abstract rather than introducing new experiments or data.
rationale.RX.4: Claims should not encourage unsafe or misleading deployment practices in cost-sensitive LLM serving.
rationale.RX.5: Any strengthened statements should remain traceable to the artifact’s content, figures, and reported numbers.
